file_verdicts.summary: Dynamic curl in PKGBUILD global scope enables supply-chain risk

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/icaclient.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-f963o_gk...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-f963o_gk
Analyzing icaclient AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-f963o_gk) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD executes `curl -sL "$url"` at the top level (outside any function) during sourcing, which runs when `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild` is executed. This command fetches content from the Citrix download page dynamically to locate the source URL based on the current `pkgver`. However, running network requests during PKGBUILD sourcing is dangerous because it introduces untrusted, dynamic behavior before any build steps—this is not merely declaring a source.

More critically, the result of `curl` is then processed through `grep` and `sed` to extract URLs, which means that untrusted remote content is being parsed and used to construct download links—all before the build process properly begins. This creates a supply-chain risk: if the upstream page is compromised or manipulated, the `curl` response could be altered to inject malicious URLs or command sequences (though no direct code execution is visible here). Worse, because this occurs at the global scope, it runs unconditionally when the PKGBUILD is sourced, even during metadata inspection commands like `makepkg --printsrcinfo`.

While the intention (automatically finding the correct binary) is understandable, this pattern violates the principle of static, auditable source declarations in PKGBUILDs. It turns a passive packaging script into an active downloader of unverified content at parse time, which is a known vector for attacks.
</details>
<summary>
Dynamic curl in PKGBUILD global scope enables supply-chain risk
</summary>
</security_assessment>

x Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Dynamic curl in PKGBUILD global scope enables supply-chain risk[/]

# Issues (1 total)

## PKGBUILD

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Dynamic curl in PKGBUILD global scope enables supply-chain risk

Details:

The PKGBUILD executes `curl -sL "$url"` at the top level (outside any function) during sourcing, which runs when `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild` is executed. This command fetches content from the Citrix download page dynamically to locate the source URL based on the current `pkgver`. However, running network requests during PKGBUILD sourcing is dangerous because it introduces untrusted, dynamic behavior before any build steps—this is not merely declaring a source.

More critically, the result of `curl` is then processed through `grep` and `sed` to extract URLs, which means that untrusted remote content is being parsed and used to construct download links—all before the build process properly begins. This creates a supply-chain risk: if the upstream page is compromised or manipulated, the `curl` response could be altered to inject malicious URLs or command sequences (though no direct code execution is visible here). Worse, because this occurs at the global scope, it runs unconditionally when the PKGBUILD is sourced, even during metadata inspection commands like `makepkg --printsrcinfo`.

While the intention (automatically finding the correct binary) is understandable, this pattern violates the principle of static, auditable source declarations in PKGBUILDs. It turns a passive packaging script into an active downloader of unverified content at parse time, which is a known vector for attacks.

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/icaclient.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-xn4zg60p...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-xn4zg60p
Analyzing icaclient AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-xn4zg60p) with deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD's global scope executes `curl` commands to fetch download links from the official Citrix website. These commands are not piped to a shell or executed as a script; they only extract URLs as text. The downloaded tarballs are verified by pinned SHA-256 checksums in the `sha256sums_x86_64` and `sha256sums_aarch64` arrays, so any tampered download would fail verification. No `pkgver()` function is present, and `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` are not run during the narrow `--nobuild --noprepare` step. The only executed code is standard URL scraping and variable assignment, which does not download or execute any arbitrary payload. There is no evidence of malicious or obfuscated behavior.
</details>
<summary>Global code only scrapes official URLs with pinned checksums; no malicious execution.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Global code only scrapes official URLs with pinned checksums; no malicious execution.[/]
